steps.instructions: Visually inspect accessible portions of the lift mechanism for misalignment, bent components, or loose hardware. Expected: Components appear aligned and secure. Why it matters: Mechanical misalignment can cause uneven lifting without actuator failure. If loose hardware → tighten if appropriate and retest.

CCR.Complaint: Table not raising properly and uneven during lift.
CCR.Cause: No external power, control, or obstruction issues found; suspected internal actuator failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for actuator evaluation/repair.

helpfulDetails: Load conditions at time of failure
Final device status (removed from service or functional)

title: Midmark 411 / 417 Series Exam Table - Backrest Not Adjusting or Stuck
description: Backrest not moving, stuck in position, or only partially adjusting due to control, obstruction, or power-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated. Why it matters: Unexpected movement or inability to reposition can cause patient injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to adjust the backrest using all available controls (hand control, footswitch if equipped). Expected: No movement, partial movement, or abnormal response. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is consistent and isolated to the backrest.

steps.title: Check Power to the Table
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is plugged into a known working outlet. Test outlet if needed. Expected: Table has stable power (other functions may operate normally). Why it matters: Insufficient power can prevent actuator movement. If no power → restore power and retest. If other functions work → proceed to next step.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control / Footswitch
steps.instructions: Check for stuck, unresponsive, or damaged buttons—especially the backrest control. Expected: Buttons depress normally and return without sticking. Why it matters: A failed or stuck control can prevent signal to the actuator. If control is faulty → replace and retest.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the backrest hinge and surrounding areas for paper roll interference, pinched upholstery, and foreign objects or debris. Expected: Backrest moves freely with no visible obstruction. Why it matters: Physical blockage is a common and easily missed cause. If obstruction found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Cable Connections (External/Accessible Only)
steps.instructions: Check visible cables between the control and table base (if accessible without disassembly). Expected: Connections are secure with no visible damage. Why it matters: Loose or damaged cables can interrupt control signals. If loose → reseat and retest. If damaged → remove from service.

steps.title: Listen for Actuator Response
steps.instructions: Activate the backrest control and listen for motor noise. Expected: Audible motor/actuator sound when engaged. Why it matters: Helps determine if the actuator is receiving power. No sound → likely control, wiring, or power issue. Sound but no movement → possible mechanical bind or actuator failure.

steps.title: Check for Load or Positioning Issues
steps.instructions: Ensure no excessive weight or uneven pressure is applied to the backrest. Expected: Backrest moves under normal load conditions. Why it matters: Overloading can prevent actuator movement. If overloaded → remove load and retest.

CCR.Complaint: Backrest not adjusting on Midmark 411 exam table.
CCR.Cause: Backrest actuator unresponsive after confirming power and control function.
CCR.Resolution: Removed table from service and sent for actuator evaluation/replacement.

helpfulDetails: Presence or absence of actuator noise
Final device status (operational or removed from service)

title: Midmark 411 / 417 Series Exam Table - Hand Control or Footswitch Failure
description: Table not responding to hand control or footswitch due to power, connection, or external control-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Loss of control can result in unsafe positioning or unexpected movement.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to operate all table functions (hi-lo, backrest) using both the hand control and footswitch. Expected: No response, intermittent response, or only one control working. Why it matters: Determines if the issue is isolated to one control or system-wide. If one control works → suspect the non-working control. If neither works → proceed with power/system checks.

steps.title: Check Power to the Table
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is plugged into a known working outlet. Test the outlet with another device if needed. Expected: Table has consistent power (indicator lights or audible relay activity if applicable). Why it matters: No power = no control response. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Connection
steps.instructions: Check for loose plugs, frayed wiring, or damage at the cord entry point. Expected: Cord intact and securely connected. Why it matters: Intermittent or lost power can mimic control failure.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control and Footswitch Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure both controls are fully seated in their ports (typically under the table or base). Expected: Secure, tight connections with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose or partially connected cables are a common failure point. Reseat connectors and retest.

steps.title: Check for Visible Damage to Controls
steps.instructions: Inspect cables, buttons, and housings for cracks, pinched wires, or fluid intrusion. Expected: No visible damage; buttons move freely. Why it matters: Physical damage can prevent signal transmission. If damaged → remove defective control from service.

steps.title: Test Controls Independently
steps.instructions: Disconnect one control and test operation using the other (if both are present). Expected: Table responds with at least one control. Why it matters: Helps isolate whether the issue is with a specific control or the system. If one works → replace the non-working control. If neither works → continue.

steps.title: Check for Stuck or Continuous Activation
steps.instructions: Inspect footswitch and hand control for stuck buttons or debris causing continuous input. Expected: Buttons return to neutral position when released. Why it matters: A stuck input can disable or override other commands. Clean or free stuck buttons and retest.

steps.title: Perform Power Reset
steps.instructions: Unplug the table, wait 30–60 seconds, then reconnect power. Expected: System resets and controls respond normally. Why it matters: Clears minor control or communication faults.

CCR.Complaint: Table not responding to hand control or footswitch; unable to adjust height or backrest. No response from either control on exam table.
CCR.Cause: Loose hand control connection found under table base. No external faults found; suspected internal control board issue.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated connector, verified full function with both controls. Removed from service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Which control failed (hand, foot, or both)

title: Midmark 411 / 417 Series Exam Table - Table Instability or Frame Looseness
description: Troubleshooting table wobbling or instability due to loose hardware, uneven surfaces, or structural issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: An unstable table presents a fall and injury risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Apply light pressure to different areas (head, seat, foot) and observe for wobble or shifting. Expected: Instability reproduced and localized if possible. Why it matters: Helps determine if the issue is global or isolated to a section.

steps.title: Check Floor Surface and Leveling
steps.instructions: Ensure the table is on a flat, stable surface. Inspect for uneven flooring or debris under feet/base. Expected: Table sits evenly with all contact points stable. Why it matters: Uneven floors are a common and easily overlooked cause of instability. If uneven → reposition table and retest

steps.title: Inspect Leveling Feet or Base Supports
steps.instructions: Check adjustable feet (if equipped) for proper contact and tightness. Ensure none are missing or worn. Expected: All feet firmly contacting the floor and secure. Why it matters: Improper leveling creates rocking or shifting during use. If loose or misadjusted → adjust and retest

steps.title: Check for Loose External Hardware
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible bolts, screws, and fasteners at base/frame connections, backrest pivot points, and side rails or accessories. Use appropriate tools to gently check for looseness (do not overtighten). Expected: All visible hardware secure with no movement. Why it matters: Vibration over time can loosen hardware and compromise stability. If loose → tighten and retest

steps.title: Inspect Backrest and Moving Sections
steps.instructions: Check if instability occurs specifically when adjusting the backrest or sections. Expected: Sections remain firm when locked in position. Why it matters: Loose pivots or locking mechanisms can mimic overall table instability. If isolated to a section → document and continue evaluation

steps.title: Check for Frame Damage or Wear
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the frame for bent metal, cracks, worn joints, and separation at weld points. Expected: Frame is intact with no visible structural damage. Why it matters: Structural damage cannot be corrected through adjustment and affects safety. If damage found → remove from service immediately

steps.title: Test Under Simulated Load
steps.instructions: Apply controlled weight (without a patient) to simulate normal use and observe stability. Expected: Table remains stable under load with no shifting or flexing. Why it matters: Confirms whether instability occurs only under load conditions.

CCR.Complaint: Exam table reported as wobbling and unstable during patient use.
CCR.Cause: Loose frame mounting bolts and uneven floor contact identified.
CCR.Resolution: Tightened hardware, adjusted leveling feet, and confirmed stable operation under load.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Midmark 411 / 417 Series Exam Table - Power Supply or Internal Wiring Faults
description: Troubleshooting guide for exam tables not powering on, intermittent operation, or loss of functions caused by external power issues, cord problems, or connection faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the table and operate all functions (hi-lo, backrest).

steps.title: Check Power Source (Outlet)
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is plugged into a known working outlet. Test with another device.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Check entire cord for cuts, fraying, crushed sections, or loose plug.

steps.title: Check Power Entry Point on Table
steps.instructions: Inspect where the power cord enters the table base and move cord to check for intermittent power.

steps.title: Verify Control Activation
steps.instructions: Attempt to operate the table using the hand control or footswitch.

steps.title: Check for Indicators (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Look for any indicator lights, sounds, or signs of power when plugged in.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections and Accessories
steps.instructions: Reseat detachable controls or accessory connections.

steps.title: Cycle Power
steps.instructions: Unplug the table, wait 30–60 seconds, and plug back in.

CCR.Complaint: Exam table not powering on or intermittently losing power during use.
CCR.Cause: Damaged power cord near base entry point causing intermittent power loss.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and replaced power cord; verified proper operation.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds, smell, or heat
Final device status (in service / out of service)

title: Midmark M9 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Unit Not Reaching Temperature or Pressure
description: Cycle runs but fails to reach proper temperature or pressure due to water, sealing, loading, or external setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sterilizer from active use. Expected: Backup sterilization method available. Why it matters: Incomplete sterilization directly risks patient safety.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle and observe temperature/pressure readings and any alarms. Expected: Unit fails to reach setpoint or alarms out. Why it matters: Confirms the issue and identifies when failure occurs (heat-up vs. pressure phase).

steps.title: Check Water Reservoir Level and Quality
steps.instructions: Inspect the reservoir and confirm it is filled with distilled water to the correct level. Expected: Proper level of clean distilled water. Why it matters: Steam generation depends on adequate, clean water. If low/empty → refill and retest. If contaminated → drain, refill with distilled water, and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Chamber Loading
steps.instructions: Ensure instruments are not overloaded and are arranged to allow steam circulation. Expected: Proper spacing between items, no overpacking. Why it matters: Overloading prevents proper steam penetration and temperature rise. If overloaded → reduce load and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Door Closure and Gasket Condition
steps.instructions: Check that the door is fully closed and the gasket is clean, intact, and properly seated. Expected: Tight seal with no visible gaps or steam leakage. Why it matters: Loss of pressure is commonly caused by poor sealing. If debris present → clean gasket and sealing surface. If damaged (cracked, flattened, brittle) → replace gasket.

steps.title: Check for Steam Leaks During Operation
steps.instructions: Run a cycle and observe around the door and fittings for escaping steam. Expected: No visible steam leaks. Why it matters: Any leak prevents pressure buildup. If leak observed → address gasket or sealing issue.

steps.title: Verify Unit is Level and Properly Installed
steps.instructions: Check that the sterilizer is on a level surface with proper clearance. Expected: Unit sits level with no tilt. Why it matters: Improper leveling can affect water distribution and sensor readings. If not level → adjust and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Source Stability
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit is plugged directly into a dedicated, functioning outlet. Avoid power strips. Expected: Stable, uninterrupted power. Why it matters: Inconsistent voltage can affect heating performance. If unstable → correct power source and retest.

steps.title: Observe Heating Behavior
steps.instructions: During cycle start, listen for normal heating sounds and monitor if temperature rises at all. Expected: Gradual increase in temperature and pressure. Why it matters: No temperature rise suggests heater or control-related issue. If no heating → likely internal component issue.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer not reaching temperature/pressure during cycle.
CCR.Cause: Door gasket worn causing pressure leak.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced gasket and verified normal cycle operation.

title: Midmark M9 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Door Not Sealing or Gasket Leaking
description: Door not sealing, visible steam leaks, or failure to build pressure due to gasket condition, debris, alignment, or closure issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sterilizer from active use if sterilization integrity is in question. Expected: Backup sterilization method available. Why it matters: Improper sealing prevents sterilization and poses patient safety risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Start a cycle or observe during operation for steam leaks, pressure alarms, or incomplete sealing. Expected: Visible steam escape, inability to reach pressure, or door not fully seating. Why it matters: Confirms a true sealing issue versus a sensor or cycle-related problem.

steps.title: Inspect Door Gasket Condition
steps.instructions: Open the door and inspect the gasket for cracks, flattening, brittleness, or debris buildup. Expected: Gasket is smooth, flexible, and free of damage. Why it matters: Worn or contaminated gaskets are the most common cause of sealing failure. If dirty → clean with mild detergent and lint-free cloth, then retest. If damaged → replace gasket and retest.

steps.title: Check for Debris on Door and Chamber Mating Surfaces
steps.instructions: Inspect the door face and chamber lip for residue, scale, or foreign material. Expected: Clean, smooth contact surfaces. Why it matters: Even small debris can prevent a proper seal under pressure. If debris found → clean surfaces and retest.

steps.title: Verify Proper Door Closure Technique
steps.instructions: Close and latch the door fully, ensuring even pressure and proper engagement. Expected: Door closes flush with no gaps and latch secures firmly. Why it matters: Improper closure can mimic gasket failure. If not fully closing → retrain user and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Door Alignment and Hinges
steps.instructions: Check for uneven gaps, misalignment, or loose hinges that prevent uniform sealing. Expected: Door aligns evenly with chamber opening. Why it matters: Misalignment prevents even gasket compression. If loose hardware → tighten accessible fasteners and retest.

steps.title: Check for Overloading or Improper Loading
steps.instructions: Verify that trays or instruments are not interfering with the door closure. Expected: No obstruction preventing full door seating. Why it matters: Improper loading can prevent full seal and cause leaks. If overloaded → correct load and retest.

steps.title: Observe During Cycle for Leak Location
steps.instructions: Run a cycle and visually identify where steam escapes (top, sides, hinge area). Expected: No visible steam leakage during pressurization. Why it matters: Leak location helps differentiate gasket vs alignment issues. If leak is uniform → likely gasket issue. If localized → possible alignment or surface defect.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer door leaking steam and failing to reach pressure.
CCR.Cause: Door gasket worn and flattened, preventing proper seal.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced gasket, cleaned sealing surfaces, and verified proper operation with successful cycle.

title: Midmark M9 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Cycle Aborts or Error Codes During Sterilization
description: Troubleshooting cycle aborts or error codes caused by sealing, water, power, or loading issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sterilizer from active use if cycle reliability is in question. Expected: Backup sterilization method available. Why it matters: Incomplete sterilization directly impacts patient safety.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a test cycle and observe when the abort occurs. Note any error codes or alarms. Expected: Cycle aborts at a consistent stage (fill, heat, pressurize, or vent). Why it matters: Identifies where in the process the failure is occurring.

steps.title: Check Power Source Stability
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit is plugged into a dedicated, working outlet. Avoid power strips or shared circuits. Expected: Stable power with no interruption during operation. Why it matters: Voltage drops or interruptions can cause cycle aborts and control errors. If unstable power is found → correct and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Door Closure and Gasket
steps.instructions: Check that the door is fully latched and the gasket is clean, intact, and properly seated. Expected: Door seals evenly with no visible gaps or steam leaks. Why it matters: Poor sealing prevents pressure buildup and commonly triggers aborts. If gasket is dirty → clean and retest. If damaged or flattened → replace gasket.

steps.title: Check Water Reservoir Level and Quality
steps.instructions: Verify the reservoir is filled with distilled water to the correct level. Inspect for debris or contamination. Expected: Proper water level with clean distilled water. Why it matters: Low or poor-quality water affects steam generation and triggers faults. If low → refill and retest. If contaminated → drain, refill, and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Load Configuration
steps.instructions: Open the chamber and check instrument loading. Ensure trays are not overloaded and steam can circulate freely. Expected: Proper spacing between instruments and trays. Why it matters: Overloading prevents proper steam penetration and may cause cycle failure. If overloaded → correct load and retest.

steps.title: Check Drain Tube and External Drain Path
steps.instructions: Inspect the drain tubing for kinks, elevation issues, or blockage. Ensure proper routing. Expected: Free-flowing drain path with no obstruction. Why it matters: Improper drainage can cause pressure or cycle errors. If obstructed → correct and retest.

steps.title: Observe Indicator Lights and Display Behavior
steps.instructions: Monitor for flickering display, inconsistent readings, or repeating error codes. Expected: Stable display and consistent error reporting. Why it matters: Helps distinguish between external issues and possible control-related faults.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 30 seconds, and power it back on. Run another cycle. Expected: Unit resets and operates normally if issue was transient. Why it matters: Clears temporary faults or minor control glitches.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer cycle aborting mid-cycle with error code during operation.
CCR.Cause: Door gasket worn and not sealing properly, causing pressure loss.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced gasket, verified proper seal, and confirmed successful cycle completion.

title: Midmark M9 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Chamber Not Draining Properly
description: Water remaining in chamber, slow draining, or cycle issues caused by drain blockage, improper setup, or external drainage problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sterilizer from service and ensure alternate sterilization is available. Expected: No instruments delayed for patient care. Why it matters: Improper drainage can result in incomplete sterilization.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle or inspect the chamber after completion. Expected: Standing water or slow drainage observed. Why it matters: Confirms an actual drainage issue vs normal residual moisture.

steps.title: Check Unit Level and Installation
steps.instructions: Verify the sterilizer is on a flat, level surface with proper rear clearance. Expected: Unit sits level with no tilt. Why it matters: These units rely on gravity for proper drainage. If not level adjust and retest.

steps.title: Inspect External Drain Tube
steps.instructions: Check the rear drain tubing for kinks, pinching, or elevation above the drain port. Expected: Tubing is straight, unobstructed, and routed downward. Why it matters: Any restriction will prevent proper water flow. If kinked or elevated correct routing and retest.

steps.title: Check Drain Container or Facility Drain Setup
steps.instructions: Ensure the drain bottle or facility drain is not full or obstructed. Expected: Drain container empty or facility drain flowing freely. Why it matters: Back pressure prevents proper drainage. If full or blocked empty or clear and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Chamber Drain Strainer (If Accessible)
steps.instructions: Look inside the chamber for debris near the drain opening. Expected: No visible debris or blockage. Why it matters: Instruments, packaging, or residue can block flow. If debris present remove safely and retest.

steps.title: Run a Test Cycle After Corrections
steps.instructions: Operate a full cycle and observe drain performance. Expected: Chamber drains completely with no standing water. Why it matters: Confirms resolution under normal operating conditions. If resolved return unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer chamber not draining; water remains after cycle.
CCR.Cause: Drain tubing found kinked behind unit restricting water flow.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned drain tubing and verified proper drainage during test cycle.

title: Midmark M9 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Control Board or Keypad Failure
description: Unresponsive keypad, incorrect inputs, display issues, or inability to start cycles caused by power, connection, or interface-related problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sterilizer from active use and confirm alternate sterilization is available. Expected: No impact to patient care workflow. Why it matters: A non-functional sterilizer compromises instrument sterility.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and attempt multiple keypad inputs. Observe display behavior and response time. Expected: No response, delayed response, or incorrect selections. Why it matters: Confirms true keypad/control issue vs user error.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the unit is plugged into a known working outlet. Test the outlet with another device. Expected: Stable power and consistent display illumination. Why it matters: Low or unstable power can cause keypad or display malfunction. If no power → restore power and retest

steps.title: Perform a Power Reset
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, unplug it for 30–60 seconds, then reconnect and power back on. Expected: System reboots and keypad responds normally. Why it matters: Resets minor control board or software glitches. If resolved → return to service

steps.title: Inspect Display and Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe for flickering, partial display, dim screen, or error messages. Expected: Clear, stable display with normal brightness. Why it matters: Display issues can indicate power supply or control board instability.

steps.title: Check Keypad Surface Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the keypad for damage, stuck buttons, worn membrane, or debris/liquid intrusion. Expected: Buttons move freely with no sticking or visible damage. Why it matters: Physical keypad failure is a common external cause. If stuck/damaged → clean or replace keypad and retest

steps.title: Inspect for Moisture or Contamination
steps.instructions: Check around the keypad and control panel for signs of water intrusion, residue, or cleaning fluid buildup. Expected: Clean, dry control surface. Why it matters: Moisture can short keypad contacts or affect responsiveness. If contamination found → clean, dry thoroughly, and retest

steps.title: Check for Loose External Connections (If Accessible)
steps.instructions: Inspect any accessible external connectors to the control panel (without disassembly). Expected: All visible connections secure. Why it matters: Loose connections can interrupt keypad signals.

steps.title: Observe for Error Codes or Boot Issues
steps.instructions: Watch startup behavior for abnormal delays, frozen screens, or recurring errors. Expected: Normal boot sequence with responsive interface. Why it matters: Helps differentiate between keypad failure and control board failure.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer keypad not responding and unable to start cycle.
CCR.Cause: Failed keypad membrane due to wear and contamination.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned and tested keypad; replaced keypad assembly when issue persisted.

title: Midmark QuickClean QC Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - No Display / Unit Does Not Power On
description: Troubleshooting a blank display or unpowered QuickClean caused by outlet, cord, rear switch, GFI, fuse, or internal power faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ultrasonic cleaner from use while the unit has no display or will not power on. Confirm that no instrument-processing cycle is needed immediately, and direct staff to another approved cleaning process or working ultrasonic cleaner according to facility policy. If fluid is present, use caution around the power connection. Do not energize the unit if there is visible leakage, fluid near electrical components, a burnt odor, unusual heat, or cord damage. Expected outcome: The unit is safely available for evaluation without creating an electrical or instrument-processing risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the reported symptom: Display remains completely blank. Unit does not respond to keypad input. Rear power switch does not illuminate, if applicable. No normal startup indication occurs. Ask staff whether the issue occurred after cleaning, moving the unit, a power interruption, a tripped GFI, or a fluid spill. Expected outcome: A true no-power or no-display condition is confirmed rather than an interrupted cycle or operational misunderstanding.

steps.title: Inspect the Unit Externally Before Applying Power
steps.instructions: Inspect the ultrasonic cleaner for: Damaged or wet power cord. Loose power inlet connection. Cracked housing or display area. Fluid leakage beneath or around the unit. Burnt smell, discoloration, or evidence of overheating. Do not proceed with powered testing if electrical damage, leakage, or overheating is present. Expected outcome: No visible condition is found that would make continued troubleshooting unsafe.

steps.title: Verify the Power Cord Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm that the power cord is fully seated at the rear power inlet and firmly connected to the wall outlet. If the cord is detachable and a known-good compatible cord is available, substitute it for testing. Expected outcome: The unit powers on and the display illuminates normally. If resolved: Return the original damaged or unreliable cord from service as appropriate, document the correction, and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check the Rear Main Power Switch
steps.instructions: Place the rear main power switch in the ON position and observe whether the switch illuminates and whether the display starts. Midmark identifies the main power connection and rear main power switch as initial checks for a QuickClean with no display. Expected outcome: The illuminated power switch and LCD display indicate normal startup. If resolved: Confirm basic unit operation while empty of instruments, document the finding, and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Test the Electrical Outlet and GFI Protection
steps.instructions: Verify that the outlet is functional using an approved outlet tester or another appropriate test method. If connected to a GFI-protected outlet, check whether the GFI has tripped. Reset it only when safe and in accordance with facility practice. When possible, test the unit on a known-good compatible outlet to determine whether the issue follows the cleaner or remains with the outlet. Midmark lists a tripped or weak/faulty GFI outlet among possible causes of a no-power condition. Expected outcome: The unit powers on from a known-good outlet, identifying the original outlet or GFI as the likely cause. If resolved: Keep the unit on an approved functional outlet and report the failed outlet or GFI for facilities/electrical correction.

steps.title: Inspect the Accessible Power-Entry Fuse Condition, When Applicable
steps.instructions: With the unit unplugged and drained or positioned safely, inspect the externally accessible rear power-entry fuse location, if present on the specific QuickClean configuration. Check for an open fuse only using approved Clinical Engineering procedures. Replace a failed fuse only with the exact approved type and rating. Midmark identifies rear bath/IEC fuses as a possible cause of a QuickClean no-display or no-power condition. Expected outcome: Correctly rated replacement fuses restore normal display and power operation without immediately failing again. If resolved: Perform a functional verification and document the fuse replacement. If a replacement fuse opens again: Stop testing. A repeated fuse failure suggests an internal electrical fault requiring repair evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Final External Function Check
steps.instructions: If power and display operation return, verify that: The display remains stable. Keypad controls respond normally. No unusual noise, heat, odor, or leakage is present. The unit can begin and stop a test cycle according to department practice, using proper fluid level and no patient-care instruments. The QuickClean uses a digital LCD control panel and includes fluid-level sensing, so a stable display and safe basic cycle response are appropriate functional observations following a power-related correction. Expected outcome: The cleaner demonstrates normal basic operation and can be returned to service according to departmental policy.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Midmark QuickClean QC Series ultrasonic cleaner had a blank display and would not begin a cleaning cycle.
CCR.Cause: Unit remained without display operation after confirming a working outlet, secure power cord connection, rear power switch position, and normal external fuse condition.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the ultrasonic cleaner from service, applied an Out of Service label, and routed the unit for bench evaluation of the internal power/display circuit.

helpfulDetails: GFI status and whether it was reset.
Power cord condition or whether a known-good cord was substituted.
Accessible fuse condition and replacement rating, if replaced.
Presence of fluid leakage, unusual heat, or burnt odor.
Whether the display returned during testing.
Final device status: returned to service or removed for repair.

title: Midmark QuickClean QC Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Sonics Low Indication or Poor Cavitation Performance
description: Troubleshooting weak ultrasonic cleaning or a Sonics Low indication caused by loading, solution, degassing, basket, or bath-condition issues.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient and Processing Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ultrasonic cleaner from instrument-processing use when Sonics Low is displayed or cavitation performance is questionable. Do not release instruments for clinical use based on a cleaning cycle performed in a unit with unverified ultrasonic performance. Follow facility procedure for affected instrument loads, including reprocessing when indicated. Avoid reaching into the tank or handling contaminated sharps unnecessarily. Use appropriate PPE and safe instrument-handling practices. Expected outcome: Potentially ineffective pre-cleaning does not contribute to reuse of inadequately processed instruments. Why it matters: Ultrasonic cleaning supports removal of debris before subsequent reprocessing steps. Poor cavitation may reduce cleaning effectiveness.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Condition
steps.instructions: Power the unit on and confirm whether the display indicates Sonics Low or whether staff are reporting poor cleaning results without a displayed fault. Document whether the issue occurs: Immediately when a cycle starts; Only with instruments loaded; Only after extended operation; With every load or intermittently; During a foil-performance test or routine cleaning observation. Expected outcome: The reported fault or reduced-performance condition is confirmed before further troubleshooting. Why it matters: Midmark identifies Sonics Low as a sonics-feedback condition that may occur when the tank is overloaded; persistent faults can require service evaluation.

steps.title: 3. Verify Power and Basic Operating Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord, rear power-switch area, and outlet connection for looseness, damage, heat discoloration, or liquid exposure. Confirm the rear power switch illuminates and that the unit completes normal startup without additional faults. When appropriate, verify the outlet with a known-working device or approved outlet tester according to department policy. Expected outcome: The unit has stable power and no external electrical condition is contributing to abnormal performance. If the issue resolves: Return the unit only after confirming normal cavitation performance and documenting the correction.

steps.title: 4. Check Tank Fill Level and Cleaning Solution
steps.instructions: With the unit not processing instruments, confirm that fluid reaches the required liquid-level sensor without exceeding the maximum fill level. Confirm that the correct cleaning agent and concentration are being used according to the solution and instrument manufacturers’ instructions. Replace visibly contaminated, heavily soiled, or incorrectly mixed solution. Use water temperature appropriate for the cleaning agent; Midmark states that cleaning efficacy can only be guaranteed when bath temperature is at least 70°F / 21°C, and water above 122°F / 50°C should not be placed into the tank. Expected outcome: The tank contains correctly prepared cleaning solution at an appropriate fill level and temperature. Why it matters: Low, contaminated, incorrectly mixed, or improperly conditioned solution can appear as poor cleaning performance even when the ultrasonic system is operating.

steps.title: 5. Run a Degas Cycle With No Instruments Loaded
steps.instructions: Remove all instruments from the tank. Confirm correct liquid level and solution concentration, place the lid on the unit, and run a degas cycle. Allow the degas cycle to continue until visible bubbles have risen from the solution; Midmark recommends a degas period of 5 to 10 minutes. End the degas cycle manually when complete. Expected outcome: Trapped air is removed from freshly prepared or recently disturbed solution before cavitation performance is evaluated. If the issue resolves: Document that performance improved after proper degassing and return the unit only after satisfactory verification.

steps.title: 6. Evaluate Loading and Basket Use
steps.instructions: Confirm that instruments are placed in the approved basket and are not resting directly on the tank bottom. Remove excessive instrument mass, tightly nested trays, stacked cassettes, or unusually heavy loads. Run a reduced-load cycle if the fault occurred only while heavily loaded. Inspect the basket feet for worn or missing protective coating. If metal is visible through worn basket-foot coating, remove that basket from use because it can damage the tank. Expected outcome: The cleaner operates with an appropriate suspended load and without basket-related tank contact. Why it matters: Midmark lists excessive tank loading as an external cause of Sonics Low and instructs that items should be suspended in the basket rather than placed on the tank bottom.

steps.title: 7. Inspect the Bath and Exterior Condition
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, disconnect power, and allow the tub to cool before inspection or cleaning. Drain the bath as appropriate and inspect the tank for: Heavy residue or contamination; Visible pitting, erosion, cracks, or leakage; Foreign objects contacting the tank; Damage caused by a worn basket; Unusual heat, odor, or sound during prior operation. Clean the empty tub only with a damp, soapy, nonabrasive cloth and rinse as appropriate. Expected outcome: The tank and basket are clean and free from visible damage that could affect performance or safe use. Why it matters: Midmark states that contaminated liquid and tank-contacting debris may reduce bath efficacy and contribute to tank wear or leakage.

steps.title: 8. Perform a Cavitation Performance Check
steps.instructions: After correcting fill level, solution, degassing, loading, and tank-condition concerns, perform a foil test according to facility procedure and applicable local requirements. Use fresh, correctly mixed solution and an unloaded basket/tank configuration appropriate for the test. Compare the result with department acceptance criteria or previous documented results when available. Expected outcome: The foil test demonstrates consistent cavitation activity across the expected cleaning area. If the issue resolves: Record the acceptable foil-test result and return the device to service according to department policy. Why it matters: Midmark identifies foil testing as a method used to evaluate ultrasonic-cleaner efficiency and recommends testing when deterioration in performance is suspected.

steps.title: 9. Stop Troubleshooting if Sonics Low or Weak Performance Continues
steps.instructions: If the unit continues to display Sonics Low, repeatedly fails a cavitation-performance check, produces abnormal sound or heat, or has visible tank damage after external causes have been addressed, discontinue further operational troubleshooting. Do not open the unit or attempt internal ultrasonic generator, transducer, or base-plate repairs as part of this guide. Expected outcome: A persistent sonics fault is escalated safely rather than allowing unreliable cleaning performance to continue.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Midmark QuickClean ultrasonic cleaner displayed Sonics Low and instruments appeared inadequately cleaned after the cycle.
CCR.Cause: Unit produced a repeated Sonics Low indication after confirming proper fill level, fresh cleaning solution, completed degas cycle, reduced basket load, and failed foil-performance verification.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ultrasonic cleaner from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented failed cavitation verification, and routed the unit for bench evaluation of the ultrasonic-generation system.

helpfulDetails: Outlet and power cord condition checked
Displayed message confirmed as Sonics Low
Fluid level at required sensor level
Basket feet inspected for exposed metal
Tank inspected for residue, pitting, or leakage
Abnormal heat, odor, or sound observed
Final device status: returned to service or removed for repair

title: Midmark QuickClean QC Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Over-Temperature Fault During Operation
description: Troubleshooting cycle interruptions or over-temperature faults caused by hot solution, insufficient fluid, excessive load, heavy use, or unresolved temperature sensing problems.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient and Staff Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ultrasonic cleaner from use during evaluation. Do not process instruments in a unit that stops during operation or displays an over-temperature condition. Allow the tank, solution, baskets, and instruments to cool before handling. Wear appropriate personal protective equipment when removing potentially contaminated instruments or solution. Expected outcome: Staff are protected from hot solution exposure, and no instrument processing depends on a cleaner with an unresolved temperature fault.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Fault and Operating Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the display reported an Over Temp, Unit Over Temp, or temperature-related fault during an active cleaning cycle. Document: Whether the fault appeared immediately or after repeated cycles. Whether the unit contained heated or unusually warm solution. Whether the tank was heavily loaded. Whether the cycle stopped or could not be restarted. Midmark identifies over-temperature conditions when tank liquid is above 60°C / 140°F. Midmark also identifies a separate unit over-temperature condition related to excessive internal base-plate temperature. Expected outcome: The reported condition is confirmed and tied to actual operating circumstances rather than an unverified complaint.

steps.title: 3. Check Solution Temperature
steps.instructions: With the unit no longer running, safely verify whether the tank solution is excessively hot. If the solution is above the unit’s normal operating range or is noticeably overheated, allow it to cool. When appropriate, drain a portion of the hot solution and replace it with properly mixed cooler solution according to department process. Do not restart the unit until the solution temperature is below 60°C / 140°F. Expected outcome: The tank solution is within an acceptable temperature range and the fault does not immediately recur. If resolved: Complete a normal test cycle with an appropriate load, confirm normal operation, document the findings, and return the device to service only when safe.

steps.title: 4. Verify Correct Fluid Level
steps.instructions: Inspect the tank and confirm it contains sufficient cleaning solution for operation. Check for signs that the unit was run with low fluid, such as solution below the required fill level or unusually rapid heating. Confirm the level-sensing area is not obstructed by debris or contamination. Midmark identifies insufficient fluid during operation as a possible contributor to a Unit Over Temp condition. Expected outcome: The tank is properly filled, the solution is contacting the level-sensing area, and the cleaner operates without overheating. If resolved: Perform a normal cycle test and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 5. Check for Excessive Instrument Load
steps.instructions: Inspect the basket load. Remove excess instruments, stacked trays, or unusually heavy contents that may overload the bath or prevent proper energy distribution. Reload the cleaner using a reasonable instrument quantity and repeat a monitored test cycle with the unit unoccupied by clinical workload. Midmark lists an overloaded bath or large instrument mass as a possible cause of a unit over-temperature condition. Expected outcome: The cleaner completes the cycle normally with an appropriate load. If resolved: Advise the department on avoiding excessive loading, document the correction, and return the unit to service.

steps.title: 6. Check for Repeated or Continuous Use
steps.instructions: Ask the department whether the unit was being used for repeated back-to-back cycles without adequate cooling time. If heavy use is reported, allow the unit and solution to cool fully, then run a monitored test cycle with proper fluid level and a normal load. Midmark identifies consistent operation without adequate time to cool as a possible cause of Unit Over Temp. Expected outcome: The unit operates normally after cooling and does not fault during a properly loaded test cycle. If resolved: Return the cleaner to service with guidance to monitor repeated-cycle operation.

steps.title: 7. Inspect External Condition and Ventilation
steps.instructions: Confirm the cleaner is installed in its normal operating location and is not surrounded by items that could trap heat or interfere with normal cooling. Inspect externally for: Blocked surrounding airflow. Excessive heat around the base or cabinet. Unusual odor, discoloration, or visible damage. Evidence of fluid leakage. Do not remove covers or perform energized internal inspection during this troubleshooting process. Expected outcome: No external installation or environmental condition is contributing to overheating.

steps.title: 8. Evaluate for an Intermittent or False Temperature Fault
steps.instructions: After the unit has cooled, the solution level is correct, and the bath is properly loaded, perform one monitored test cycle. If the unit again reports an over-temperature fault despite normal fluid temperature, proper loading, and reasonable use, suspect an internal temperature-sensing, control, or ultrasonic generator cooling problem. Midmark’s troubleshooting information identifies sensor-related temperature faults when abnormal temperature readings occur during a cycle. Expected outcome: A repeat fault under normal conditions confirms the problem is not corrected by external adjustments.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Midmark QuickClean QC Series ultrasonic cleaner stopped during operation and displayed an over-temperature fault.
CCR.Cause: Found the tank solution excessively hot after repeated back-to-back cycles with a heavily loaded basket; fault did not recur after cooling and correcting the load.
CCR.Resolution: Allowed the unit to cool, restored proper operating conditions, completed a successful monitored cleaning-cycle test, and returned the cleaner to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact displayed message: Over Temp, Unit Over Temp, or temperature sensor-related fault.
Solution temperature at time of evaluation.
Any unusual heat, smell, leakage, or visible damage.
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service.

title: Midmark QuickClean QC Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Touchpad or Cycle Controls Not Responding
description: Troubleshooting unresponsive touchpad buttons or cycle controls caused by moisture intrusion, power interruption, display status, or touchpad failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ultrasonic cleaner from instrument-processing use until the controls respond reliably. Do not process instruments in a unit that cannot start, stop, or adjust a cycle as intended. Remove instruments from the basket using appropriate sharps precautions and arrange alternate cleaning equipment if needed. Expected outcome: No contaminated instruments depend on an ultrasonic cleaner with unreliable cycle controls. Why it matters: Unreliable controls may prevent proper cleaning-cycle completion or safe interruption of operation.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Control Failure
steps.instructions: With the unit not in clinical use, identify which controls are not responding: START / stop-cycle control; MENU control; UP / ON / YES control; DOWN / OFF / NO control; All touchpad buttons. Confirm whether the LCD display is active, whether a cycle is running, and whether any message appears on the screen. Expected outcome: The failure is identified as an isolated button problem, complete touchpad failure, or broader power/display issue. Why it matters: Midmark identifies the QuickClean controls as START, MENU, UP/ON/YES, and DOWN/OFF/NO functions; an inactive display may indicate a different problem than an unresponsive touchpad.

steps.title: Verify Power and Normal Display Operation
steps.instructions: Check that: The power cord is fully connected at the cleaner and wall outlet; The rear power switch is in the ON position and illuminated; The outlet is functional using an approved outlet test method; The LCD screen is displaying normal startup or cycle information. If the unit has no power or no display, address the power/display condition before evaluating touchpad response. Expected outcome: The cleaner powers on normally with an active LCD display. Why it matters: Midmark lists loss of power, rear power-switch position, and power-supply conditions as causes of a no-display complaint.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Cleaning-Solution Intrusion at the Touchpad
steps.instructions: Inspect the touchpad and surrounding control-panel surface for: Liquid residue; Recent spill evidence; Cleaning solution around the button membrane; Cracked, lifted, punctured, or peeling touchpad material; Moisture tracking from the tank or lid area toward the controls. Do not continue repeatedly pressing controls if fluid intrusion is suspected. Power the unit off, unplug it, and document the observed condition. Expected outcome: The touchpad surface is dry and intact, or fluid intrusion/damage is identified. Why it matters: Midmark’s troubleshooting chart lists fluid entering the touchpad as a cause of touchpad buttons not responding and directs replacement of the touchpad after eliminating the fluid source.

steps.title: Clean and Dry the External Control Surface Safely
steps.instructions: If no obvious damage is present and only external residue is seen: Turn the unit off; Disconnect it from power; Allow the unit to cool if recently operated; Wipe the external touchpad area with a damp soapy cloth, avoiding excess liquid; Dry the surface completely; Reconnect power and retest the controls. Do not spray cleaner directly onto the touchpad or allow liquid to run into the control-panel area. Expected outcome: Controls respond normally after removal of external contamination. If resolved, return to service only after confirming normal cycle operation. Why it matters: Midmark instructs that cleaning be performed with the unit switched off and disconnected from power, using a damp soapy cloth.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Unloaded Control Test
steps.instructions: With the tank appropriately filled and no instruments loaded: Power the unit on; Confirm that the display does not show a low-fluid condition; Press MENU and verify that the settings screen changes; Press the UP and DOWN controls and verify that settings respond; Return to the start screen; Press START to begin an unloaded test cycle; Confirm that the cycle starts and can be stopped normally. If the display indicates insufficient fluid, correct the water level before attempting a cycle. Expected outcome: All touchpad controls respond and the cleaner begins and ends a cycle normally. Why it matters: QuickClean units use a fluid sensor that can prevent cycle operation when water level is insufficient; this must be ruled out before concluding that the touchpad is defective.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Failure Is Intermittent or Persistent
steps.instructions: Repeat the basic control test several times while observing for: Buttons that respond only with excessive force; Delayed response; Certain buttons functioning while others do not; Controls failing after exposure to moisture or after a cycle; Display functioning normally while button input is absent. Do not flex, puncture, peel, or disassemble the touchpad during this evaluation. Expected outcome: The touchpad is confirmed either reliable or intermittently/persistently defective.

steps.title: Evaluate for Removal from Service
steps.instructions: Remove the unit from service when: Any control remains unresponsive; Touchpad response is intermittent; Fluid intrusion or membrane damage is visible; A cycle cannot be reliably started or stopped; The display operates normally but touchpad input is not accepted. Expected outcome: A cleaner with unreliable controls is not returned to instrument-processing use.

CCR.Complaint: Sterile processing reported that the Midmark QuickClean QC Series powered on, but the START and MENU touchpad controls would not respond.
CCR.Cause: Unit displayed normally and had adequate fluid level, but multiple touchpad buttons remained unresponsive; moisture residue was observed along the control-panel membrane.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the ultrasonic cleaner from service, labeled it Out of Service, and routed it for bench repair due to suspected fluid-damaged touchpad assembly.

helpfulDetails: Rear power switch illuminated: Yes / No
Specific buttons affected: START / MENU / UP / DOWN / All
Moisture or cleaning-solution residue observed: Yes / No
Touchpad membrane damage observed: Yes / No
Cycle successfully started and stopped: Yes / No
Final device status: Returned to Service / Out of Service / Sent for Repair

title: Midmark QuickClean QC Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Tank, Drain, Valve, or Plumbing Leakage
description: Troubleshooting visible fluid leakage from the tank, drain hose, drain valve, plumbing connections, or a damaged/pitted ultrasonic bath.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ultrasonic cleaner from clinical use as soon as leakage is reported or observed. Turn the unit off, unplug it from AC power, and prevent staff from running additional cleaning cycles until the leak source is identified. Do not handle spilled cleaning solution without appropriate PPE, and clean any fluid from surrounding counters or floors according to facility procedure. Expected outcome: The unit is electrically isolated, and staff are protected from fluid exposure, slip hazards, and possible electrical risk. Why it matters: A leaking ultrasonic cleaner may allow solution to reach electrical components or create unsafe environmental contamination.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Leak Condition
steps.instructions: Ask staff when the leak occurs: While the tank is filled but idle; Only during an ultrasonic cleaning cycle; While draining the tank; After the drain valve is closed; Continuously from beneath or behind the unit. Dry the exterior surfaces and surrounding counter area. With the unit unplugged, visually inspect for evidence of prior leakage, including dried residue, staining, moisture tracks, or pooled solution. Expected outcome: The leak pattern is identified before additional testing. If no evidence of leakage is found: Document the reported condition and proceed with a controlled fill observation before returning the device to use.

steps.title: 3. Inspect the Drain Hose and Discharge Routing
steps.instructions: Check the externally accessible drain hose for: Cracks, splits, pinholes, stiffness, or discoloration; Loose connection at the drain outlet; Kinking, pulling, or tension on the hose; Improper routing that allows fluid to run back toward the unit; Drain hose end positioned where overflow or splashback may be mistaken for a unit leak. The QuickClean QC Series uses an external drain hose and shutoff valve arrangement, with the hose size and configuration varying by model and installation type. Expected outcome: The drain hose is intact, securely routed, and not the source of leakage. If the hose is visibly damaged or leaking: Keep the unit out of service and replace the affected drain component through appropriate repair channels. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 4. Check the Drain Valve Position and External Valve Area
steps.instructions: Confirm the drain valve is fully closed before filling the tank. Inspect the accessible valve area and surrounding fitting for moisture, residue, looseness, or active dripping. With the unit still unplugged, add a small controlled amount of water sufficient to observe the drain outlet and valve area without exceeding normal operating limits. Watch for leakage with the valve closed. Expected outcome: No dripping occurs from the drain valve or external drain connection while the valve is closed. If fluid drips from the valve, outlet, or associated fitting: The valve or plumbing connection is not sealing correctly. Remove the unit from service and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

steps.title: 5. Check for Overfill, Spillover, or Misidentified Leakage
steps.instructions: Inspect the tank fill level and ask whether the leak was observed after the bath was recently filled, moved, or loaded with instruments. Confirm that: The fluid level was not above the indicated maximum fill level; Instruments or baskets were not displacing solution over the rim; Fluid did not spill down the exterior during filling or draining; The unit was not operated with visible solution on the case. Expected outcome: Overflow or handling-related fluid is ruled out as the apparent leak source. If the reported “leak” was caused by overfill or spillover: Clean and dry the unit, verify normal operation with a controlled fill, educate staff as appropriate, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: 6. Inspect the Tank Interior for Visible Damage
steps.instructions: After the tank is drained, cool, and dry, inspect the bath interior under good lighting. Look for: Pitting or roughened areas in the bottom of the tank; Pinholes, cracks, or corrosion-like damage; Wear marks where the basket may have contacted the tank; Damaged or missing protective coating on the basket feet. Midmark states that contaminants contacting the vibrating bath can act as abrasives and, in extreme cases, cause the bath to develop holes and leak. Midmark also identifies severe cavitation erosion and excessive pitting in the tank base as causes of fluid leakage requiring replacement of the ultrasonic cleaner. Expected outcome: The tank interior is smooth and intact, with no visible evidence of erosion or perforation. If tank damage, pitting, or a suspected pinhole is present: Remove the unit from service. Do not attempt to seal or continue using the bath. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 7. Perform a Controlled Static Fill Observation
steps.instructions: Only when no obvious damaged hose, faulty valve, or tank defect has been identified, place the unplugged unit on a clean, dry surface with absorbent material positioned beneath and behind the cleaner. Add water to the normal operating level. Leave the unit unplugged and observe the unit for active moisture from: The tank underside or case seam; The drain valve area; The drain hose connection; Any accessible external plumbing location. Expected outcome: The unit remains dry while filled and stationary. If leakage occurs while the unit is unplugged and idle: The issue is mechanical, involving the tank, drain valve, hose, or plumbing connection. Remove the unit from service and stop.

steps.title: 8. Determine Whether the Leak Is Cycle-Related
steps.instructions: Do not operate the unit if any leak has already been confirmed. If the unit remains completely dry during the unplugged static fill observation, and the original report occurred only during operation, the problem may be an intermittent leak, vibration-related plumbing issue, or fluid displacement during loading. Run an operational test only when permitted by department procedure, with the device isolated from clinical use and monitored continuously. Stop the cycle immediately if any moisture appears. Expected outcome: No solution escapes during operation or draining. If fluid appears during vibration or draining: Remove the unit from service and send it for bench evaluation. Midmark’s troubleshooting information identifies plumbing joints and severe tank cavitation erosion as primary causes of fluid leakage.

steps.title: 9. Verify Final Condition Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: Return the device to service only when: No active leak is found during fill, cycle, and drain observation; The tank is visually intact; The drain valve closes and drains normally; The external drain hose is secure and undamaged; The unit and surrounding area remain dry; The reported cause has been identified and corrected, such as overfill or a loose external drain-hose setup. Expected outcome: The cleaner operates without leakage and can be safely returned to clinical processing use. If the leak source cannot be confidently identified: Do not return the unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported that the Midmark QuickClean QC Series ultrasonic cleaner was leaking cleaning solution onto the counter during tank draining.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found active dripping from the drain valve area with the valve closed, while the tank and external drain hose showed no visible damage.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the ultrasonic cleaner from service, labeled it Out of Service, drained and cleaned the unit, and sent it for bench evaluation of the drain valve and associated plumbing components.

helpfulDetails: Whether leakage occurred while filled, operating, or draining
Drain valve position when leakage was observed
Tank interior condition, including pitting or visible damage
Location of pooled fluid or residue
Whether leakage occurred during static fill observation
Whether leakage occurred only during a cleaning cycle
